compiled.raw:
You're building a checkout system. Your service handles credit cards — fine. Then the product team adds PayPal. Then Apple Pay. Then split-payments across a card and a wallet. Then a "buy now, pay later" provider your CEO signed last quarter.

Your `PaymentService.process()` method is now 340 lines of `if (method === "credit-card") ... else if (method === "paypal") ...`. Every time one payment provider changes their API, three other payment providers almost break. Every new intern fears that file.

You sense there's a shape here — a way to arrange these classes so new methods slot in without the existing ones flinching. You've felt it before, in other codebases. You don't have a name for it yet.

That feeling — *"there's a shape here"* — is what a design pattern names.

We'll start with the one that matches your checkout problem. But first: what is a pattern, really?

A design pattern is a recurring **arrangement of classes and responsibilities** that solves a specific design problem. Three parts of that definition do load-bearing work:

### Recurring

A pattern isn't a clever trick someone invented. It's a shape that shows up in codebase after codebase, because a class of problems shows up over and over. If you'd never seen it before but had ten years of OO experience, you'd probably invent it yourself. That's a good sign.

### Arrangement

A pattern is not a library, not a specific algorithm, not a language feature. It's the *relationship between classes*. <Class id="Strategy">Strategy</Class>, the one we're about to meet, is just "one interface and a family of classes that implement it" — but naming that arrangement turns a pile of code into a conversation.

### Specific problem

Every pattern comes with a precise problem statement and a non-trivial trade-off. If a pattern solves ten problems at once, it's not a pattern — it's a vibe.

The Gang of Four (Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, John Vlissides, 1994) cataloged 23 of them. Modern practice uses more — the architectural patterns like Repository and Saga, the concurrency patterns like Producer-Consumer, the failure patterns like Circuit Breaker. But the 23 are the vocabulary you'll reach for every week.

<Callout kind="aside">
Why not just read the GoF book? Because patterns taste different in code you actually own. You'll remember three you use more than twenty you read about. We'll build the vocabulary one pattern at a time.
</Callout>

widget.prompt: Which of these is a design pattern, not just a technique?

options.label: Caching results to speed up repeated calls
options.whyWrong: Caching is a general technique — no specific arrangement of classes, no defined responsibilities. You cache values, you don't arrange classes.

options.label: Keeping exactly one shared instance of a class
Using a hash table instead of a linked list for O(1) lookup
options.whyWrong: That's a data-structure choice, not a pattern. Patterns are about class arrangement, not which container you pick.

options.label: Catching exceptions and retrying on transient failures
options.whyWrong: Retry is a technique — sometimes it's wrapped into a Circuit Breaker pattern, but on its own it's just a loop.

compiled.raw:
Back to your checkout. Here's what you're going to do:

1. Define one interface — <Class id="PaymentStrategy">PaymentStrategy</Class> — with one method, `charge(amount, customer)`.
2. Write one class per payment method: <Class id="CreditCardStrategy">CreditCardStrategy</Class>, <Class id="PayPalStrategy">PayPalStrategy</Class>, <Class id="ApplePayStrategy">ApplePayStrategy</Class>. Each implements `PaymentStrategy.charge` its own way.
3. In your checkout service, hold a reference to a `PaymentStrategy` and call `.charge()`. You don't know or care which one.

That's Strategy. Three classes where you had one 340-line method, and each class does one thing you can test, replace, or version independently.

### The shape

- One <Concept id="interface">interface</Concept> says *what* happens.
- Many classes say *how* it happens.
- A caller picks *which* at runtime.

The name "Strategy" comes from the idea that each implementation is a **strategy** for accomplishing the same goal. Different strategies produce different results, but the caller treats them uniformly.

### What you gain

Adding a new payment method is now: *write one class*. Nothing else touches. You've turned a risky change into a boring one. That's the prize patterns pay out over time.

### What you pay

Two extra classes minimum (interface + concrete) and a new indirection. If your checkout only ever supports credit cards, you paid for nothing. Patterns are tools, not decorations.

widget.prompt: Which of these are signs that Strategy fits? Select all that apply.

options.label: You have a family of algorithms or implementations that do "the same thing" differently.
You want to save memory by sharing state across instances.
options.whyWrong: That's Flyweight — a structural pattern for dedup, not behavior swap. We'll meet it in Unit 8.

options.label: Your big switch/if chain is growing every sprint, tied to one input type.
You need exactly one shared instance across the app.
options.whyWrong: That's Singleton. Single instance is the problem; Strategy doesn't care how many instances exist.

options.label: You want to swap implementations at runtime without a restart.
You need to record user actions so you can undo them.
options.whyWrong: That's Command + Memento — together, not apart. We'll meet both in Unit 11.

compiled.raw:
A pattern you applied too early costs more than one you missed.

Strategy on your 340-line payment method: clear win. Strategy on a `formatDate()` function that only ever handles ISO-8601: you paid for two classes, an interface, and a dependency-injection wiring — to replace four lines.

Three tests before reaching for a pattern:

### 1. Can I name the problem?

If you can't write one sentence that starts *"I have a recurring choice between ..."* you don't have the problem a pattern solves.

### 2. Is it actually recurring?

Not hypothetical. You've seen this shape in this codebase at least twice, or you reasonably expect to see it in the next six months. If not, write the obvious code.

### 3. Would the pattern survive scrutiny?

If a new engineer reads this code, do they think *"oh, that's a nice arrangement"* or *"why are there so many files"*? The second is a smell.

<Callout kind="warn">
The junior-developer failure mode is to apply Strategy everywhere. The senior-developer failure mode is to never apply it — to write one more 20-line switch because "we'll refactor later." Aim for the middle.
</Callout>

Strategy is a good first pattern because the shape is small. In Unit 4 we'll meet its cousins — <Class id="FactoryMethod">Factory Method</Class>, <Class id="Builder">Builder</Class>, <Class id="Observer">Observer</Class> — and you'll start to see which arrangement fits which itch.

sections.title: See you in a few days

params.recap:
Strategy is one interface plus a family of implementations, picked at runtime.
Cost is two extra classes. Win is that new implementations drop in without
touching old ones. Reach for it when the choice is recurring and the selection
is runtime-dynamic.

cards.front: A family of algorithms or implementations doing 'the same thing' differently, swappable at runtime.
What's the primary cost of the Strategy pattern?
cards.back: Two extra classes per algorithm family (interface + at least one implementation), plus the wiring to pick one — paid in code weight, earned back in boring changes.
